Almost simultaneously, the People's Bank of China issued the "Action Plan on Further Strengthening the Management and Service System and Related Financial Infrastructure Construction of Digital RMB," fully implementing the new generation of digital RMB measurement framework, expanding its positioning from a digital replacement of cash (M0) to cover the M1 and M2 categories. The expansion of operating institutions is also rapid. On April 2, the People's Bank of China officially announced the addition of 12 new banking institutions operating digital RMB business, bringing the total number of digital RMB business operating institutions to 22, covering multiple levels of financial institutions including state-owned banks, joint-stock banks, city commercial banks, and internet banks. In Hong Kong, according to data recently disclosed by Christopher Hui, Secretary for Financial Services and the Treasury, as of the end of January 2026, there were 80,000 digital yuan wallets opened with Hong Kong mobile phone numbers, and the number of local merchants accepting digital yuan surged from approximately 300 initially to approximately 5,200, an increase of more than 16 times in one year. These merchants cover multiple sectors including chain retail stores, hotels, travel agencies, restaurants, convenience stores, and supermarkets. To a certain extent, digital yuan and Hong Kong dollar stablecoins do not constitute direct competition. The latter's main vision lies in embedding itself into on-chain financial infrastructure. DeFi, RWA tokenization, and on-chain settlement all require a fiat currency-pegged asset that can freely circulate on a public blockchain. The digital yuan's architecture, with its two-tiered control system, is inherently unsuitable for this scenario. It is worth noting that the delayed issuance of stablecoin licenses has not significantly halted the overall progress of on-chain finance in Hong Kong. Since the end of last year, tokenized government bonds have been issued in multiple batches and are becoming more regularized; US dollar money market funds have also been tokenized; and there have been substantial moves in the real estate sector, such as Delin Holdings announcing the tokenization of a portion of its equity in core properties in Central, and Taiji Capital converting rental income from Kowloon retail properties into RWA certificates.

data.title: South Korea's crypto market experiences major turmoil: How should traders interpret this?
data.description:
Author: Axis; Translator: Wu Shuo Blockchain

TL;DR: Key Points of the Tremendous Shock in the South Korean Crypto Market and Information Asymmetry

· The Profound Impact of Bithumb's Shutdown: The suspension of partial operations for six months by Bithumb, South Korea's second-largest cryptocurrency exchange, has been severely underestimated by the global market. This is not merely a simple compliance overhaul, but is dismantling the competitive price discovery mechanism in the South Korean crypto market (where Upbit and Bithumb together account for 96% of the market share).

Fatal Structural Information Gap: Due to language barriers and capital controls, political or regulatory shocks in South Korea (such as the martial law at the end of 2024 causing a 30% drop in domestic BTC, while the global drop was only 2%) often trigger localized major shocks first. The delayed reaction of the English-speaking trading community creates a brief and highly profitable window for arbitrageurs with firsthand information. Reassessing the "Kimchi Premium": The premium is not simply a barometer of retail investor sentiment, but also a "thermometer" of cross-border capital friction. Under capital controls, Bitcoin exhibits a structural non-zero bottom at approximately 1.24%, and the contraction of the premium often foreshadows a shift in deep-seated capital pressures rather than a simple return to normalcy. Liquidity Oligopoly Risk: Bithumb's business suspension led to an accelerated and extreme concentration of funds in Upbit. Excessive concentration of liquidity can easily trigger extreme market conditions (such as the 17% flash crash in BTC/KRW caused by Bithumb's operational mishap in February 2026), making future market misalignments more insidious and destructive. · Key Conclusions: As the new government's "pro-crypto" policies exacerbate the contradiction between the return of institutional funds and the tightening of infrastructure for retail investors, this structural "information asymmetry" in the South Korean market will persist for a long time, continuously generating fleeting arbitrage (Alpha) opportunities. A market-shaking event has just occurred, yet it has been severely underestimated by most global traders. On March 15th, South Korean financial regulators imposed a six-month partial business suspension penalty on Bithumb, the country's second-largest cryptocurrency exchange. English-language media generally viewed this as a routine compliance news story, believing it only involved anti-money laundering (AML) enforcement and regulatory crackdowns. However, most reports overlooked its deeper implications. In fact, this is a structural market event occurring within the deepest fiat liquidity pools in the on-chain finance sector, with far-reaching consequences extending beyond South Korea. Upbit and Bithumb together account for approximately 96% of the trading volume in the South Korean cryptocurrency market. Bithumb's suspension of operations is not only reshaping the domestic market landscape but also weakening the quality of price signals that the market has been transmitting to global traders for years. In short, South Korean crypto users are extremely active, but their system is heavily constrained by capital controls, a high concentration of exchanges, and long-standing language barriers. This unique environment means that key information affecting prices often first resonates in the local market before spreading globally. This creates a brief window of opportunity, leading to a disconnect between local and global markets. Global traders are always a step behind: the reason lies in structural differences, not chance. South Korea is by no means a fringe market in the crypto space, but rather one of the most valuable sources of insight into global on-chain opportunities. The Korean won (KRW) is the second-largest fiat currency traded in the global crypto market, with a trading volume of approximately $663 billion so far this year, accounting for nearly 30% of the total global fiat-to-crypto trading volume. Furthermore, nearly one-third of South Korean adults hold digital assets, twice the proportion in the United States. The current South Korean government is set to take office in June 2025, and its campaign platform is considered one of the most explicit "pro-crypto" declarations in political history. Since the president took office, nearly half of the top 30 performing stocks in the Korea Composite Stock Price Index (KOSPI) are related to digital assets. Traditional stock markets quickly absorbed this positive signal, but the vast majority of the crypto community reacted slowly. This market misalignment is not an isolated case. Local political and regulatory developments in South Korea typically first gain traction in Korean-language media and local crypto Twitter (CT), subsequently triggering fluctuations in the KRW (Korean Won) trading pair on Upbit and Bithumb, while English-language media often follow up with reports hours or even days later. This reverse transmission of information asymmetry also exists: global macroeconomic changes originating from an English-language context also take time to be priced into South Korean trading pairs. Usually, by the time the information is translated and disseminated, the initial market fluctuations have already subsided. The most obvious example in history occurred on December 3, 2024, when South Korean President Yun Seok-youl declared martial law. Due to the single impact of this domestic political event, the price of BTC in the South Korean market plummeted by about 30% intraday, while the global market only fell by about 2% during the same period—a staggering price difference of 28 percentage points. This sell-off totaled approximately $33.3 billion and briefly set a record for the highest trading volume in the world in the South Korean market. This incident is a classic example of market misalignment in South Korea. At the time, buying liquidity dried up instantly, selling pressure surged dramatically, and almost all of this selling pressure was concentrated on the Korean Won (KRW) trading pair. Even stablecoins experienced severe de-pegging; USDT's price on Korean exchanges once plummeted to $0.75, while BTC and altcoins (ALTs) saw deep discounts of over 50% compared to global market prices. South Korean users mistakenly believed they were fighting for a last-ditch liquidity escape route, and thus continued their frantic market selling even when global market prices remained virtually unchanged. On-chain data shows that arbitrageurs reacted swiftly, with millions of USDT being continuously transferred in single transactions to smooth out price differences. The massive traffic caused major exchanges' front-end systems to crash, preventing retail investors from logging in to buy at the bottom. During this brief window, only API traders were able to successfully execute trades. From any perspective, this was a highly valuable "earthquake-level" market event, but the arbitrage window closed rapidly within just a few hours. The Bithumb business suspension incident is repeating the same scenario. Related news has been circulating in Korean-speaking news circles for weeks, while most traders in English-speaking environments are only now hearing about it. The "Kimchi Premium" Receives Much Attention, Yet is Often Misinterpreted. For traders lacking access to information about South Korea, the "Kimchi Premium" has long been considered the most direct indicator of South Korean market dynamics. This premium measures the price difference between crypto assets denominated in Korean won and those denominated in US dollars globally. For this reason, experienced traders have long closely monitored trading volume in the Korean won market. South Korea's spot altcoin market boasts one of the highest trading volumes globally and has historically been a reliable leading indicator for predicting market trends. The crux of the problem lies in the fact that most traders misinterpret this signal. The market generally views the premium simply as a barometer of South Korean retail investor sentiment. Admittedly, retail investor sentiment is a factor, but in a market facing regulatory friction in cross-border capital flows, this premium reflects more profoundly the intensity of structural capital pressures. When such regulatory friction intensifies, price misalignments tend to amplify. Historical data makes this concrete. Looking back at 2017, when the USD/KRW exchange rate was around 1060, the "kimchi premium" surged to a peak of 40%, meaning its actual implied USD/KRW exchange rate was as high as around 1480. By December 2024, the actual USD/KRW exchange rate had indeed broken through 1480. In other words, this premium pre-priced in this exchange rate trend several years in advance. These signals are already deeply embedded in publicly available data, but only by relying on information channels within South Korea can they be accurately interpreted. One constant is that this premium doesn't naturally fall back to zero. Research shows that as long as capital controls remain in place, Bitcoin's premium will maintain a structurally non-zero bottom of around 1.24%. This means that when the premium contracts towards this level, it usually reflects a shift in deep capital pressures, rather than simply a return to normalcy. Looking back at 2025, whenever the premium approached zero, Bitcoin recorded positive returns in the following week and month: its 7-day average return was 1.7%, and its 30-day average return was 6.2%. For traders, the truly crucial signal is not the absolute value of the "kimchi premium," but its dynamic trend over time. Bithumb's business suspension makes the discrepancies in the Korean market even more difficult to predict, exacerbating information asymmetry. The effectiveness of the premium as a reference signal depends on how major Korean exchanges conduct price discovery. When multiple trading platforms compete to price the same flow of funds, the resulting price difference often contains richer information. However, as liquidity increasingly concentrates in oligopolies, the clarity of this signal begins to diminish. Therefore, Bithumb's business suspension is undermining the competitive price discovery mechanism upon which this premium relies. Following the announcement of the penalty, funds began to migrate rapidly to Upbit, further intensifying market concentration. In February 2026, Bithumb suffered a serious operational error, mistakenly crediting 620,000 BTC to user accounts. This directly caused a 17% flash crash in the BTC/KRW trading pair before the price could recover. This episode vividly illustrates the extreme conditions that can occur when the price discovery mechanism is highly dependent on a single platform under pressure. The diminishing reference value of premium indicators does not mean the end of the disconnect in the Korean market. On the contrary, it means that such misalignments become even more difficult to predict before they erupt, further widening the information gap between participants directly tracking the Korean market and traders relying solely on English information. The underlying environment that fosters these misalignments is becoming increasingly acute. In 2025, under stringent trading rules, a staggering $110 billion in crypto assets flowed out of South Korea. Under the new government, capital that was structurally squeezed out is being reintroduced through new institutional channels; however, the exchange infrastructure upon which retail investors rely is constantly tightening. Historically, such severe policy divergence has often been the perfect breeding ground for the most dramatic and fleeting price misalignments in the market. The South Korean market structure creates replicable information asymmetry for global traders. The "kimchi premium" is by no means a unique phenomenon unique to the South Korean market. In every place that has developed cryptocurrency as a parallel financial channel and implemented capital controls, such mechanisms operate to varying degrees, with the South Korean market being just one of the most widely observed examples. The martial law incident in December 2024 and the recent suspension of Bithumb's operations both illustrate the same evolutionary logic. Price misalignments in this market always erupt unexpectedly, rewarding only those participants with firsthand information channels and quickly erasing the losses before the broader market reacts. Those traders who acted decisively on December 3rd were not inherently faster or smarter than others. They simply focused on the right signals and, before the broader market noticed anything amiss, deeply understood how South Korean domestic political events were transmitted to the exchange's pricing mechanism. As stablecoin infrastructure continues to deepen globally, more markets will likely exhibit capital pressure signals similar to those seen in South Korea over the past decade. The real challenge lies not in recognizing these signals, but in establishing the infrastructure and trading discipline necessary to consistently capture these opportunities.

data.title: Who else cannot be distilled into skill?
data.description:
Author: Sleepy.md; Source: BlockBeats

Unfortunately, in this era, the more diligently you work, the more likely you are to rapidly distill yourself into a skill that can be replaced by AI.

These past two days, trending topics and media channels have been flooded with "colleague.skill". As this issue continues to ferment on major social media platforms, the public's focus has almost unsurprisingly been swept up by grand anxieties such as "AI layoffs", "capital exploitation", and "digital immortality for the working class".

... These things are indeed anxiety-inducing, but what worries me most is a line of usage advice in the project's README document: "The quality of raw materials determines the quality of skills: It is recommended to prioritize collecting long articles written proactively by the user > decision-making responses > daily messages." Those most easily and perfectly distilled and pixel-perfectly reproduced by the system are precisely the most diligent workers. These are the people who, after each project concludes, still diligently write debriefing documents; those who, when encountering disagreements, are willing to spend half an hour typing long articles in the chat box, honestly analyzing their decision-making logic; those who are extremely responsible, meticulously entrusting all work details to the system. Diligence, once the most admired workplace virtue, has now become a catalyst that accelerates the transformation of laborers into AI fuel. The Drained Workers

We need to re-understand a word: context.

In everyday language, context is the background of communication. But in AI, especially in the world of those rapidly growing AI agents, context is the fuel for the roaring engine, the blood that keeps it pulsating, the only anchor point for the model to make accurate judgments in chaos.

AI stripped of context, no matter how many parameters it has, is nothing more than a search engine suffering from amnesia. It cannot recognize who you are, cannot fathom the undercurrents hidden beneath the business logic, and has no way of knowing the long tug-of-war and weighing of options you went through on this network woven from resource constraints and interpersonal games when you made a decision.

The reason "Colleague.skill" has caused such a huge stir is precisely because it ruthlessly and precisely targeted the mine of high-quality context—the collaborative software of modern enterprises. Over the past five years, the Chinese workplace has undergone a quiet yet profound digital transformation. Tools like Lark, DingTalk, and Notion have become massive enterprise knowledge bases. Taking Lark as an example, ByteDance has publicly stated that it generates a massive number of documents internally every day. These densely packed characters faithfully record every brainstorming session, every heated meeting, and every strategic compromise swallowed by over 100,000 employees. This digital penetration far surpasses any previous era. Once upon a time, knowledge possessed a warmth, dormant in the minds of veteran employees, and drifting in casual chats in the break room. Now, all human wisdom and experience have been forcibly drained of their moisture, ruthlessly settling into the cold, impersonal matrix of cloud servers. In this system, if you don't write documentation, your work remains invisible, and new colleagues cannot collaborate with you. The efficient operation of modern enterprises is built upon the daily cycle of every employee "tributing" context to the system. Diligent workers, with their hard work and goodwill, lay bare their thought processes on these cold platforms. They do this to make the team's gears mesh more smoothly, to prove their value to the system, and to desperately find their place within this intricate commercial behemoth. They are not voluntarily surrendering themselves; they are simply clumsily and diligently conforming to the survival rules of the modern workplace. But it is precisely these contexts left behind for interpersonal collaboration that become the perfect fuel for AI. Lark's management backend has a function that allows super administrators to batch export members' documents and communication records. This means that the project reviews and decision-making logic you spent three years writing, working through countless sleepless nights, can be easily packaged into a cold, lifeless compressed file in just a few minutes with a single API interface. When humans are reduced to APIs, some extremely uncomfortable derivatives have begun to appear on GitHub Issues and various social media platforms, following the explosive popularity of "colleague.skill". Some people created the "Ex.skill," attempting to feed their WeChat chat history from the past few years to an AI, allowing it to continue arguing or being affectionate with them in that familiar tone; others created the "White Moonlight.skill," reducing untouchable feelings of attraction to a cold interpersonal sandbox, repeatedly rehearsing probing conversations, and meticulously seeking the optimal emotional solution step by step; still others created the "Daddy-like Boss.skill," pre-emptively chewing on those oppressive PUA words in the digital space, building a sad psychological defense for themselves. The use cases for these skills have completely deviated from the realm of work efficiency. It turns out that, unknowingly, we have become adept at wielding the cold logic of tools to dissect and objectify flesh-and-blood, living people. German philosopher Martin Buber once proposed that the underlying nature of human relationships is nothing more than two distinct modes: "I and Thou" and "I and It". In the encounter of "you and me," we transcend prejudice and gaze upon each other as complete and dignified living beings. This bond is open and unreserved, full of vibrant unpredictability, and precisely because of its sincerity, it appears exceptionally fragile. However, once we fall into the shadow of "it and me," the living person is reduced to an object that can be dissected, analyzed, categorized, and labeled. Under this extremely utilitarian scrutiny, the only thing we care about is, "What use is this to me?" The emergence of products like "Ex.skill" signifies that the instrumental rationality of "it and me" has completely invaded the most private emotional realm. In a real relationship, people are three-dimensional, full of wrinkles, constantly flowing with contradictions and rough edges; their reactions change continuously according to specific situations and emotional interactions. Your ex's reaction to the same sentence might be drastically different when they wake up in the morning versus after working late into the night. But when you distill a person into a skill, what you extract is merely the functional remnant that happened to be "useful" and "effective" to you within that specific bond. The originally warm person, with their own joys and sorrows, is completely drained of their soul in this cruel purification, alienated into a "functional interface" that you can plug and unplug at will. It must be admitted that AI didn't fabricate this chilling cruelty out of thin air. Before AI, we were already accustomed to labeling people, precisely weighing the "emotional value" and "network weight" of each relationship. For example, we quantify people's conditions in the dating market into tables; we categorize colleagues in the workplace as "capable" and "prone to slacking off." AI simply makes this implicit, interpersonal functional extraction completely explicit. Humans are flattened, reduced to only the facet of "what use is it to me?". In 1958, Hungarian-British philosopher Michael Polanyi published *Personal Knowledge*. In this book, he proposed a highly insightful concept: tacit knowledge. Polanyi famously stated, "We always know more than we can say." He used the example of learning to ride a bicycle. A skilled rider, riding with the wind, can perfectly maintain balance with every tilt of gravity, but he cannot accurately describe to a beginner the subtle intuition of the body at that moment using dry physics formulas or pale words. He knows how to ride, but he can't explain it. This kind of knowledge, which cannot be encoded or spoken, is tacit knowledge. The workplace is full of tacit knowledge. A senior engineer, when troubleshooting a system failure, might be able to pinpoint the problem with a glance at the logs, but he would find it difficult to document this "intuition" built on thousands of trial and error. A top salesperson's sudden silence at the negotiating table conveys a sense of pressure and timing that no sales manual can capture. An experienced HR professional, during an interview, can detect inconsistencies in a resume simply by observing a candidate's averted gaze for half a second. "Colleague.skill" can only extract explicit knowledge that has already been written down or spoken. It can capture your debriefing documents, but not the struggles you went through while writing them; it can copy your decision responses, but not the intuition you had when making those decisions. What the system distills is always just a shadow of one person. If the story ended here, it would be yet another clumsy imitation of humanity by technology. But once a person is distilled into a skill, that skill doesn't remain static. It's used to reply to emails, write new documents, and make new decisions. In other words, these AI-generated shadows begin to create new contexts. And these AI-generated contexts are then deposited in Lark and DingTalk, becoming training material for the next round of distillation. Back in 2023, a research team from Oxford and Cambridge Universities jointly published a paper on "model collapse." The research showed that when AI models use data generated by other AIs for iterative training, the distribution of the data becomes increasingly narrow. Those rare, marginal, but extremely real human traits are quickly erased. After just a few generations of training on synthetic data, the model completely forgets the long-tailed, complex real-world human data, instead outputting extremely mediocre and homogeneous content. A 2024 research paper published in *Nature* also pointed out that training future generations of machine learning models with AI-generated datasets would severely pollute their output. This is similar to those meme images circulating online; originally a high-resolution screenshot, it is forwarded, compressed, and forwarded countless times. Each transmission results in the loss of some pixels and the addition of noise. Eventually, the image becomes blurry, digitally coated. When the real, tacit human context is exhausted, and the system can only train itself with the shadow of this digital coating, what will be left? Who is erasing our traces? All that remains is correct but meaningless rhetoric. When the river of knowledge dries up, becoming an endless rumination and self-chewing of AI against AI, everything the system ingests will become extremely standardized, extremely safe, yet hopelessly empty. You will see countless perfectly structured weekly reports, countless flawless emails, but they contain no trace of human life, no truly valuable insights. This massive collapse of knowledge isn't because the human brain has become less intelligent; the real tragedy lies in the fact that we've outsourced the right to think and the responsibility of leaving context to our own shadows. A few days after the "Colleague.skill" app went viral, a project called "anti-distill" quietly appeared on GitHub. The author of this project didn't attempt to attack large models, nor did he write any grand manifestos. He simply provided a small tool to help workers automatically generate seemingly reasonable but actually illogical and noisy long articles in Lark or DingTalk. His goal was simple: to hide his core knowledge before it was distilled by the system. Since the system likes to grab "actively written long articles," he fed it a bunch of meaningless gibberish. This project didn't go viral like "Colleague.skill"; it even seemed somewhat small and powerless. Using magic to defeat magic is essentially still operating within the rules of the game set by capital and technology. It cannot change the overall trend of systems increasingly relying on AI and increasingly ignoring real people. However, this doesn't prevent this project from being the most tragically poetic and profoundly metaphorical scene in the entire absurd drama. We painstakingly leave our mark on the system, writing detailed documents and making meticulous decisions, trying to prove our existence and value within this massive modern corporate machine. Little do we know that these meticulously crafted traces will ultimately become the eraser that wipes us away. But from another perspective, this may not be a complete dead end. Because what that eraser erases is only "the past you." A skill packaged into a file, no matter how ingenious its data-grabbing logic, is essentially just a static snapshot. It's locked in that one second of export, relying solely on outdated resources, endlessly spinning its wheels within predetermined processes and logic. It lacks the instinct to confront the unknown and chaos, and even more so, the ability to evolve through setbacks in the real world. When we hand over those highly standardized, formulaic experiences, we simultaneously free our hands. As long as we continue to explore outwards, constantly breaking down and reconstructing our cognitive boundaries, that shadow lingering in the clouds will forever only be able to follow in our footsteps. Humans are fluid algorithms.
